title: Client Roaming Event Analysis
dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:client`. from the `client` modular input in the Cisco Catalyst Add-on (Splunkbase 7538) polling `GET /dna/intent/api/v1/client-detail`. Default poll interval: 900s (15m). Volume: ~1 event per client per poll (can be very high volume). RBAC: NETWORK-ADMIN-ROLE. Key fields: `macAddress` (string), `hostType` (string), `connectionType` (string), `healthScore{}` (object), `ssid` (string), `vlanId` (string), `location` (string).
spl: index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:client" connectionType="WIRELESS" | stats count as roam_count avg(roamDuration) as avg_roam_ms by macAddress, ssid | where roam_count > 3 | eval avg_roam_ms=round(avg_roam_ms,0) | sort -avg_roam_ms | head 20
description: Tracks wireless client roaming events to identify devices with excessive roaming or slow roam times that degrade real-time application performance.
value: Excessive or slow roaming disrupts voice and video calls. Identifying problematic clients and areas enables AP placement and configuration optimization.
implementation: Enable the `client` input in the Cisco Catalyst TA pointing to `index=catalyst`. The TA polls client detail from the Catalyst Center Intent API on a typical 60-minute interval. Key fields: `macAddress`, `connectionType`, `ssid`, and `roamDuration` (and any dedicated roam or mobility fields your TA version extracts in the `cisco:dnac:client` payload).
visualization: Table (macAddress, ssid, roam_count, avg_roam_ms), time series of roam events if timechart added in a follow-on panel, top-N list of slowest roamers.

references.title: Catalyst Center Wireless RF Profile API — Cisco DevNet

knownFalsePositives:
**Sticky client not roaming despite being in a better-AP zone.** Some client devices have aggressive sticky-client behavior, maintaining their association with the original AP even when a closer AP is available. The lack of roaming events for these clients is expected device behavior. Distinguish by checking whether `roamType` is absent for clients with high `rssi` variance. This is not a false positive — the lack of roaming is the finding itself.

**802.11r/k/v fast roaming creating rapid succession of roam events.** In environments with 802.11r Fast BSS Transition enabled, roaming events happen within milliseconds and may generate multiple events per roam attempt (pre-authentication, re-association, key exchange). Distinguish by checking whether multiple roam events occur within 1 second for the same `clientMacAddress`. Suppress by deduplicating roam events within a 2-second window per client.

**Client device with aggressive roaming threshold creating excessive roam events.** Enterprise devices with aggressive roaming settings (e.g., roam at -65 dBm instead of -72 dBm) will roam more frequently, generating more events. Distinguish by checking whether specific `clientMacAddress` or `deviceType` values have disproportionately high roam counts. Suppress by setting per-device-type roaming frequency baselines rather than a global threshold.

**AP load balancing triggering client reassociation.** Catalyst Center's AP load balancing feature may actively move clients from overloaded APs to less-loaded ones, generating roaming events that are infrastructure-initiated rather than client-initiated. Distinguish by checking whether the `roamReason` indicates load balancing rather than signal-driven roaming. Do not suppress — these are legitimate roaming events, but they should be categorized separately from client-initiated roams.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cisco Catalyst Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 7538) with the **client** modular input enabled, writing to `index=catalyst` with `sourcetype=cisco:dnac:client`.
• Catalyst Center **2.3.5+** with Assurance licensing for wireless client detail including roaming events.
• 802.11r (Fast BSS Transition), 802.11k (neighbor reports), and/or 802.11v (BSS transition) enabled on the wireless network for meaningful roaming analysis.
• RBAC: service account with **NETWORK-ADMIN-ROLE**.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
The TA polls the client detail API at the configured interval. Roaming events are captured when a client transitions from one AP to another.

Key fields for roaming event analysis:
• `roamType`: type of roam — "802.11r" (fast roam), "802.11i" (full re-authentication), "CCKM" (Cisco Centralized Key Management).
• `previousApName`: the AP the client was associated with before roaming.
• `apName`: the AP the client roamed to.
• `macAddress`: client MAC address.
• `rssi`: RSSI at the new AP after roaming — should be better than the previous AP.
• `roamTime`: time taken for the roaming process (milliseconds). 802.11r roams should complete in <50ms; full re-authentication roams may take 200ms+.
• `ssid`: SSID — roaming should occur within the same SSID.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:client" connectionType="WIRELESS" isnotnull(previousApName)
| eval roam_event=1
| stats count as roam_count dc(macAddress) as roaming_clients avg(roamTime) as avg_roam_time_ms values(roamType) as roam_types by apName, siteId
| sort -roam_count
```

Understanding this SPL:
• **`isnotnull(previousApName)`**: Filters to roaming events only — clients that transitioned from a previous AP to a new AP. Initial associations do not have a previous AP.
• **`stats ... by apName, siteId`**: Groups roaming events by destination AP. APs with very high roam counts may be in high-traffic areas or may be pulling clients due to better signal (load balancing).
• **`avg(roamTime)`**: Average roaming time per AP. High average roam times indicate clients are not using fast roaming protocols (802.11r) and may experience application disruption during roams.
• **`values(roamType)`**: Shows which roaming protocols are used. If only "802.11i" (full re-auth) appears, 802.11r may not be configured.

Step 3 — Validate
• **UI comparison:** Open Catalyst Center > Assurance > Health > Client Health > [select a wireless client]. Check the "Connection Timeline" for roaming events. Compare the roam count and type with the Splunk results.
• **Roaming quality check:** Filter for `roamTime > 200` to identify slow roams. Cross-reference with the AP pair (from → to) to identify problematic AP transitions.
• **Protocol check:** Verify that the expected roaming protocol (802.11r) is being used. If all roams show as "802.11i", check the wireless profile configuration in Catalyst Center.

Step 4 — Operationalize
• **Dashboard:** Roaming frequency heatmap by AP, average roam time distribution, and a table of client-AP pairs with the highest roam counts (may indicate RF coverage overlap issues or sticky client behavior).
• **Alert:** Trigger when `avg_roam_time_ms > 500` for any AP — indicates roaming performance degradation that may impact real-time applications (voice, video).
• **RF optimization:** High roaming frequency between specific AP pairs may indicate excessive RF overlap. Consider reducing transmit power or adjusting AP placement.

Step 5 — Troubleshoot
• **No roaming events detected:** The `previousApName` field may not be populated in all Catalyst Center versions. Check for alternative fields like `connectedApPreviousName` or `lastConnectedAp`.
• **Roam times appear unreasonably high:** Catalyst Center may report the total reconnection time (including DHCP renewal) rather than just the 802.11 roaming time. Use the roamType to determine the expected duration.
• **Too many roaming events from a single client:** The client device may have aggressive roaming settings. Check the `macAddress` against known device types — some enterprise laptops and phones have configurable roaming thresholds.
• If data is not arriving for `cisco:dnac:client`, check that the `the corresponding` input is enabled in the TA configuration and that the Catalyst Center API credentials have not expired.
grandmaExplanation: We track how devices move between wireless access points and flag those that are struggling to switch smoothly, which causes calls to drop.
